hook: William O'Neil pulled the biggest winning stocks of the last hundred years, lined them up, and asked what they shared in the months before they exploded. The answer became a seven-letter system whose most important rule is the one that feels most wrong: buy the stock hitting new highs, not the cheap one in the bargain bin.

p: O'Neil was a stockbroker gripped by one question. Why do a handful of stocks climb five hundred, a thousand, even two thousand percent while most just drift sideways or sink? Rather than trade on opinion, he went digging through history, pulling the greatest winning stocks of each era back to the late eighteen hundreds and studying what their earnings, their charts, and their buyers looked like in the months right before they took off. He built detailed model books on every one.

The winners were not random. They shared seven traits before they made their giant moves, and O'Neil turned those traits into an acronym you could hold in your head, CAN SLIM. He later founded the newspaper Investor's Business Daily to hand ordinary investors the same earnings and relative strength data that Wall Street had kept to itself. This is not a theory dreamed up in an armchair. It is a pattern pulled from a century of the market's actual biggest winners.

C is current quarterly earnings. The most recent quarter's earnings per share should be up at least twenty-five percent versus the same quarter a year earlier, and far more in the true winners, with the rate of growth accelerating over recent quarters and sales rising alongside profits.

A is annual earnings growth. Look for a strong multi-year record, roughly twenty-five percent a year or better with each year higher than the last, backed by a return on equity of at least seventeen percent to prove the growth is well managed and durable, not a fluke.

N is something new. Almost every big winner had a new product, new management, or new industry conditions driving it, paired with a stock pushing to new price highs out of a sound base. What looks too high to most people usually goes higher.

S is supply and demand. Tighter share counts move faster than bloated ones, so watch the supply of stock and read demand through volume, wanting the breakout to come on trading volume at least forty to fifty percent above normal.
L is leader, not laggard. In any strong group buy the one or two true leaders with a relative strength rating of eighty or higher, never the cheaper me-too stock that only looks like a bargain.

I is institutional sponsorship. You want a growing number of quality funds buying, the fuel that drives sustained moves, but not a stock so over-owned that every institution already holds it and the buying power is spent.

M is market direction, the letter O'Neil called the most important of all. About three out of four stocks follow the general market, so only buy aggressively when the major indexes are in a confirmed uptrend.

Here is the paradox at the center of the book, the part that trips up nearly everyone. Most people want to buy a stock that has fallen a long way and looks cheap near its lows, because it feels safe. O'Neil's century of data said the opposite. The great winners launched their huge moves while making new highs, breaking out to prices they had never seen. A stock at a new high has no overhead resistance, nobody sitting on a loss waiting to sell and get even, so everyone who owns it is happy and the new high itself is telling you demand is overwhelming supply. A stock scraping along near its lows is usually cheap for a reason.

But the new high has to emerge from the right setup, which is where chart reading comes in. A base is a stretch where a stock pauses and trades sideways for weeks or months, working off prior gains and shaking out nervous holders before its next move. The most famous base is the cup with handle. Picture the silhouette of a coffee cup, a gradual rounded decline and recovery forming the cup, then a short gentle drift down on quiet volume near the top forming the handle. Two close cousins are the flat base, a tight shallow sideways band, and the double bottom, a W shape whose second low dips just under the first to flush out the last weak holders.

Every base has a pivot point, the precise price where the stock is coiled and ready to break out. You buy as it clears that pivot on a surge of volume forty to fifty percent or more above normal, and you stay disciplined, buying within about five percent of the pivot. If the stock has already run fifteen or twenty percent past the pivot before you noticed, it is extended, and you let it go rather than chasing, because your entry price is what your entire risk system is built around.
sections.pull: What seems too high and risky to most people usually goes higher, and what seems low and cheap usually goes lower.

sections.h: Cut Every Loss At Eight Percent
p: If you remember one thing from the book, O'Neil said, remember how you handle losses, because it decides whether you survive. His rule is mechanical and non-negotiable. When a stock falls seven to eight percent below what you paid, you sell it, no exceptions, no hoping, no waiting for it to come back. You take the small loss and move on, no matter how much you love the company or how sure you are it will bounce.

The reason is math and human nature. Losses are not symmetric. An eight percent loss needs less than a nine percent gain to recover, easy enough, but a loss allowed to run to fifty percent needs a hundred percent gain, a double, just to get back to even, and deeper than that the hole becomes almost impossible to climb out of. One catastrophic loss can erase a dozen good decisions. And since you bought right at the pivot of a proper base, a correct buy should not fall eight percent, so the loss itself is the signal that your read was wrong. O'Neil showed you can be right only about a third of the time and still do well, as long as the losses stay tiny and the winners are allowed to grow.

This is why he was so emphatic about one thing above all, never average down. Buying more of a stock as it falls below your cost, to lower your average price, feels like grabbing a bargain, but it pours money into a position the market is actively telling you is weak. You add to winners that prove you right by rising, never to losers. Averaging down is how a small manageable loss becomes an account-destroying disaster.
sections.pull: The small loss is not a defeat. It is the insurance policy that keeps any single mistake from taking you out of the game.

p: Selling well is harder than buying, and it is where a lot of the money is won or lost. O'Neil's general guideline is to take profits on a normal winner when it is up around twenty to twenty-five percent from the buy point. That sounds modest until you see the logic. Most breakouts advance about that much and then pull back to build a new base, so you harvest the meat of the move and sidestep the correction. Pair that with cutting losses at eight percent and your average winner runs about three times your average loser, the asymmetry that lets you be wrong more often than right and still finish well ahead.

There is one big exception. Now and then you catch a true market leader destined to rise two hundred or three hundred percent, and clipping it at twenty-five percent would waste the very stock that could make your whole year. O'Neil's tell for these is speed. If a stock races up twenty percent or more in just one to three weeks after breaking out, that blazing strength suggests something exceptional, so you hold it at least eight weeks and give it room to run, using a line like the ten-week moving average as a trailing guide rather than snatching the quick profit.

How do you know even a big winner is finally topping? The signs usually appear while the stock is still climbing and the mood is euphoric, which is exactly why most people sell too late. Watch for a climax top, a stock that goes vertical and spikes further in a week or two than at any point in its whole run. Watch for huge volume with little price progress, the fingerprint of big institutions quietly distributing shares into the excitement. Watch for a break below a key moving average on heavy volume, sloppy late-stage bases that keep failing, and the market's leaders starting to break down. The idea is to sell into strength while eager buyers are still there, not to panic on the way down with everyone else.

O'Neil is adamant that the biggest obstacle between you and success is not the market, it is you. The number one account killer is the refusal to take a small loss. A stock drops eight percent, then twelve, then twenty, and at every step pride and hope will not let you admit you were wrong, so a small manageable loss grows into a devastating one. The market does not know you own the stock and does not care what you paid, so being willing to be wrong quickly and cheaply is a strength, not a weakness.

He names the other traps too. Chasing cheap low-priced stocks because you can own more shares, when the quality names are usually the higher-priced ones. Buying on tips and rumors instead of doing your own homework on the earnings and the chart. Obsessing over catching the exact bottom, holding a deteriorating stock out of fear of taxes, letting hope stand in for a plan. Notice how backwards the emotions run. Buying a leader breaking out on huge volume feels scary and too high, exactly when you should feel confident, while holding a falling loser feels comfortable and hopeful, exactly when you should be afraid.

That is why the eight percent rule is a number and not a judgment call. It takes the emotion out. O'Neil also asks you to keep records of your own trades and study them honestly, learning from your real winners and especially your losers rather than repeating the same mistakes. His whole system was pulled from what actually worked across a hundred years of the market, and it rewards the patient, the objective, and the disciplined while punishing the stubborn and the hopeful. He never promised you would be right every time. He promised that if you keep your losses tiny and let your winners run, the math and the method can carry you, and that protecting your capital always comes first.
sections.pull: The breakout feels too high exactly when you should feel confident. The falling loser feels comfortable exactly when you should be afraid.

chart.title: The Gain It Takes To Recover A Loss
chart.subtitle: Why a small loss is survivable and a big one is not
chart.caption: Illustrative. The gain needed just to climb back to break even after a loss of each size, which is exact arithmetic. O'Neil cites the eight and fifty percent cases himself. Small losses are easy to recover and deep ones are nearly impossible, which is why he cuts every loss near eight percent.

takeaways: Before considering any stock, run it through CAN SLIM: strong and accelerating current earnings, a multi-year record with high return on equity, something genuinely new, tight share supply, clear leadership, growing fund ownership, and an uptrending market.

Buy strength, not bargains. Wait for a stock to break out of a proper base like the cup with handle at its pivot point on volume forty to fifty percent above normal, and buy within five percent of that pivot instead of chasing an extended move.
Check relative strength before you buy. Favor the leader with a rating of eighty or higher and pass on the cheaper laggard sitting in the same group.
Set your exit before you enter. Sell any stock that falls seven to eight percent below your buy price, mechanically, with no exceptions and no averaging down into the loss.
Take normal profits in the twenty to twenty-five percent range, but hold a stock that races up twenty percent in one to three weeks, since that speed can mark a rare and much bigger winner.
Read the general market every day. Respect a cluster of distribution days as a warning and wait for a follow-through day before buying aggressively, because three of four stocks follow the market.
Keep a record of every trade and review your winners and your losers, so you learn from your own behavior instead of repeating it.
